coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots on the anticorrosive action of the tin coatings
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Duarte, Taiane Fíngolo
Paiva, Victor Magno
Archanjo, Braulio Soares
Muniz da Silva de Almeida, Clara
Suguihiro, Natasha Midori
Corrêa, Rodrigo José
Ribeiro, Emerson Schwingel
D'Elia, Eliane
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanomaterials with a nearly spherical shape and a size smaller than 10 nm. The literature reports on studying metallic composites using other carbon allotropes, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide. However, no studies have applied carbon quantum dots to metallic coatings. Tin is a metal widely used industrially due to its resistance to oxidation, corrosion resistance, and weldability. However, the thickness and microstructure of the material can impact its resistance. Therefore, this work studied composite coatings of tin (Sn) and CDs at different concentrations to improve the material's corrosion resistance. Different concentrations of CDs were analyzed to obtain Sn – CD composite coatings: 0.03, 0,04, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 g L−1. Mild steel plates were used as substrate using electrodeposition in galvanostatic mode with j = 6.25 mA cm−2. The performance of the coatings was evaluated by electrochemical measurements such as open circuit potential, electrochemical impedance, and potentiodynamic polarization curve. In addition, characterizations of the CDs and composite films were carried out by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Zeta Potential (ζ), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), UV-VIS Spectroscopy, Fluorimetry, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Excited Photoelectron Spectroscopy X-Ray (XPS). The electrochemical tests show an optimal working concentration equivalent to 0.05 g L−1 of CDs in the bath. The SEM and XDR show that this concentration favored the growth of the film in a more uniform manner, providing a more homogeneous barrier when compared to the pure Sn film. The XPS indicated that the pure Sn film oxidized more than the composite film, suggesting that the addition of CDs improved the resistance of this material.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology:2022
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Anticorrosive coatings
Composites coatings
Tin
Corrosion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Eliane D'Elia reports financial support was provided by Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of Rio de Janeiro State. Eliane D'Elia reports financial support was provided by National Council for Scientific and Technological Development. Taiane Fingolo Duarte reports financial support was provided by National Agency of Oil Natural Gas and Biofuels Rio de Janeiro. Eliane D'Elia reports a relationship with Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of Rio de Janeiro State that includes: funding grants. Eliane D'Elia reports a relationship with National Council for Scientific and Technological Development that includes: funding grants. Taiane Fingolo Duarte reports a relationship with National Agency of Oil Natural Gas and Biofuels Rio de Janeiro that includes: funding grants, speaking and lecture fees, and travel reimbursement. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.The authors would like to thank the collaborations with the multi-user laboratories at the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro for the availability of equipment to perform FTIR, Zeta potential, DLS, fluorimetry, XRD, and XPS analyses. Also noteworthy is the collaboration established with Inmetro, RJ - Brazil through Br\u00E1ulio Soares Archanjo and Clara Muniz da Silva de Almeida, who made the morphological and topographic analyses. The authors would like to thank the financial support granted by the Human Resources Program 20.1 (PRH 20.1), ANP, FAPERJ (E-26/211.418/2021), and CNPq (312336/2021-7 and 440299/2021-7).

The authors would like to thank the collaborations with the multi-user laboratories at the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro for the availability of equipment to perform FTIR, Zeta potential, DLS, fluorimetry, 0XRD, and XPS analyses. Also noteworthy is the collaboration established with Inmetro, RJ - Brazil through Br\u00E1ulio Soares Archanjo and Clara Muniz Almeida, who made the morphological and topographic analyses. The authors would like to thank the financial support granted by the Human Resources Program 20.1 (PRH 20.1), ANP, FAPERJ (E-26/211.418/2021), and CNPq (312336/2021-7 and 440299/2021-7).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR of CDs and Citric Acid. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs diluted in water. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs at different concentrations in water (D) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs at different concentrations in the Sn bath. (E) Emission spectroscopy of CDs. (F) Image of pure Sn solution exposed to UV light. (G) Image of CD solution exposed to UV light.
XRD for Sn – 0.05 g L−1CDs composite and Sn coatings.
(A), (B), and (C) SEM for Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CD composite film and (D), (E), and (F) SEM for Sn film.
(A) HR-TEM image, lattice fringes shown as inset, and (B) Histogram of the size distribution of carbon quantum dots.
AFM topographic image (A) for Sn and (B) for Sn 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite coatings (C) and (D) RMS for Sn and Sn 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite coatings
X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum for composite Sn – CD 0.05 g L−1 in (A), (B) and (C) and spectrum for pure Sn in (D), (E) and (F).

(A) Nyquist Diagrams. (B) Nyquist Diagrams to Sn and Sn-CD 0.05 gL–1 after 2 days in NaCl 3,5% (C) and (D) Bode Diagrams (E) Potentiodynamic polarization curves. (F) Potentiodynamic polarization curves highlighting the active, passive, and transpassive regions. (G). Equivalent circuit used simulation of the EIS data.
XRD for study corrosion product seen at anodic polarization in (A) Pure Sn and (B) Sn – CD 0.05 g L−1.
XRD for Post-Corrosion Analysis in (A) Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite and (B) Pure Sn.
X-Ray spectrum high resolution after Sn - CD 0.05 g L−1 composite corrosion in (A) Sn3d and (B) C1s and (C) X-Ray spectrum high resolution after Sn corrosion in 3d.

row.entry: Composition for Sn – 0,05 g L−1 CDs composite coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical Parameters obtained from electrochemical impedance diagrams.
X-Ray spectrum high resolution to Sn – CD 0,05 g L−1 composite and Sn.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots on the anticorrosive action of the tin coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro Campus UFRJ Duque de Caxias Prof. Geraldo Cidade, Duque de Caxias - RJ, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanomaterials with a nearly spherical shape and a size smaller than 10 nm. The literature reports on studying metallic composites using other carbon allotropes, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide. However, no studies have applied carbon quantum dots to metallic coatings. Tin is a metal widely used industrially due to its resistance to oxidation, corrosion resistance, and weldability. However, the thickness and microstructure of the material can impact its resistance. Therefore, this work studied composite coatings of tin (Sn) and CDs at different concentrations to improve the material's corrosion resistance. Different concentrations of CDs were analyzed to obtain Sn – CD composite coatings: 0.03, 0,04, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 g L−1. Mild steel plates were used as substrate using electrodeposition in galvanostatic mode with j = 6.25 mA cm−2. The performance of the coatings was evaluated by electrochemical measurements such as open circuit potential, electrochemical impedance, and potentiodynamic polarization curve. In addition, characterizations of the CDs and composite films were carried out by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Zeta Potential (ζ), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), UV-VIS Spectroscopy, Fluorimetry, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Excited Photoelectron Spectroscopy X-Ray (XPS). The electrochemical tests show an optimal working concentration equivalent to 0.05 g L−1 of CDs in the bath. The SEM and XDR show that this concentration favored the growth of the film in a more uniform manner, providing a more homogeneous barrier when compared to the pure Sn film. The XPS indicated that the pure Sn film oxidized more than the composite film, suggesting that the addition of CDs improved the resistance of this material.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by three dimensions: length, depth, and height on a nanometric scale. Within this classification, carbon quantum dots (CDs) or carbon dots (CDs) are an allotropic variation of carbon with a semi-crystalline structure and almost spherical shape, composed of a graphitic core (sp2 type bonds) and a surface with oxygenated groups, such as carbonyls, carboxyls, and hydroxyls [1]. This new carbon-based material was accidently obtained in 2004 by Xu and collaborators during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes using the electrophoresis technique. CDs are promising materials within nanotechnology. In addition to their optical, electronic, and electrochemical properties, they have good chemical stability, high solubility in water, low-cost synthesis, and low toxicity, making them considered eco-friendly materials [1,2]. Since its discovery, many theories about its luminescence have emerged, the most widespread of which can be justified by passivation, doping, and quantum confinement. CDs are semiconductors, and like all charge-carrying materials (electrons and holes), they have valence, conduction, and forbidden bands. However, unlike known semiconductors, the luminescent properties of CDs are related to particle size [3]. The electronic properties are more exposed to the crystalline structure, as its dimension is zero. Qualitatively, it is proposed that as the particle shrinks, the size of the forbidden band increases. In this way, the energy emitted by the electron moves to more energetic lengths. These characteristics enable broad applicability since different CDs can be obtained by changing synthesis parameters. Therefore, materials engineering has dedicated itself to exploring this material in nanomedicine, biomedicine, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, chemical sensors, and biosensors [2–4]. In this context, the possibility of applying this material in the field of corrosion stands out, and CDs have already shown promise. Studies of new inhibitors are being developed by synthesizing CDs and doped and co-doped CDs [5,6]. Tang et al., 2024, synthesized CDs doped with nitrogen and co-doped with nitrogen and cerium, in order to study effect this material as anti-bacterial and anti-corrosive inhibitors. The study shows that material N-CDs doped with cerium performed better than N-CDs. Huang et al., 2024, synthesized a corrosion inhibitor using citric acid as a precursor. The authors also evaluated the anticorrosive behavior when adding different concentrations of nitrogen to CDs. The study demonstrates that there is an optimal concentration of nitrogen, which performed better protective performance [7,8]. Ye et al., 2020, also studied citric acid as a precursor of CDs. The authors, as well as Huang et al., 2024, used l-histidine as a nitrogen source to dope the CDs. In this work, it was observed that the protective performance increased proportionally to the concentration of the inhibitor, presenting larger capacitive arcs in the presence of N-CDs [9]. Cen et al., 2019, studied nitrogen-doped and sulfur-co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors for aluminum alloy. The authors studied different concentrations of N, S-CDs, observing a lower corrosion rate as N, S-CDs were added until reaching a concentration of 4.0 mg L−1, suggesting this concentration as maximum inhibition [10]. Its application in composite coatings has also been studied. Even so, the composites developed are organic, being synthesized mainly with epoxy resin [11]. In the case of metallic coatings, the addition of CDs to these matrices is little explored. The literature reports studies of graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) incorporated into tin (Sn) matrices, but the use of CDs has not yet been explored. Gupta and Srivastava, 2018. Dammu et al., 2023 studied Sn-GO and Sn-CNT composites, respectively. These studies indicate that there is optimal work concentration and that these carbon allotropes change the grain microstructure, performing better anticorrosive action [12,13]. Considering that there is not much information in the literature regarding the use of CDs in metal coatings, this work sought to investigate the influence of this material on tin coatings. The tin is a non-toxic metal that presents three allotropic variations: grey tin (Sn-α), white tin (Sn-β), and rhombic tin (Sn-ɤ). Among them, Sn-β stands out, which has a face-centered tetrahedral system and is the most stable allotropic variation. Therefore, Sn-β is widely used in the food, pharmaceutical, electronics, and automobile industries and welding regions [14,15]. Given its wide industrial application, many studies have been conducted to improve this film. This metal is resistant to atmospheric oxidation, has good corrosion resistance, and is a good electronic conductor. The deposition of this material has been the subject of research since bath conditions and substrate can influence the film's microstructure, resulting in equiaxed or dendritic morphologies. These studies aim to find conditions that favor equiaxed growth, a microstructure that forms a homogeneous film. Homogeneous depositions are an effective physical barrier against the corrosive environment [16]. Due to its anticorrosive resistance and good electronic conductivity, it is widely used in electrical/electronic circuits and welding regions. Rapid technological advances have resulted in the miniaturization of electrical and electronic parts in the search for lighter and more compact technologies. These small changes can be responsible for electronic component failures since they involve thinner films [17]. Thus, Sn coatings can present problems, such as Sn pest and whisker formation in electronic circuits [10]. Another issue discussed in the literature is its deposition kinetics, which should be further studied. Depending on the conditions, this film can “burn,” presenting a dark and opaque color, which impacts the quality and aesthetics of the film. Therefore, Sn composites that improve their properties would greatly benefit the industry [14]. This work explored the addition of CDs to the Sn bath to improve its properties, suggesting a new field of study for this coating [19,20].
Citric acid (CA: C6H8O7) and the other analytical reagents were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (≥99.0%). Furthermore, ultrapure distilled water was used during the study.

CDs were synthesized from citric acid (≥99.0%). The synthesis occurred by thermal process, in which citric acid was subjected to 200 °C for 2 h. As post-treatment, filtration was carried out through a 0.22 μm membrane, followed by 48 h of dialysis. Finally, this material was dried in an oven at 70 °C. The mass obtained was used to prepare a concentrated aqueous stock of CDs. In contrast, the composition of the Sn bath was adapted from Gupta et al., 2018, and its composition is reported in Table 1 [12].

Sn bath additives improved the solution's homogeneity. Sodium acetate (99.995%) worked as a buffering agent, sodium gluconate (≥99.0%) as a complexing agent, and sodium lauryl sulfate (≥99.0%) as a surfactant [12,21].

The composite solutions were obtained from the CDs stock solution, in which different aliquots were mixed with the Sn bath (Table 1). Five concentrations of CDs were evaluated in the bath: 0.03, 0.04, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 g L−1. In all solutions, the pH remained at 4.0 [12].

The electrodepositions were carried out in galvanostatic mode under a current density of 6.25 mA cm−2 [12]. A mild steel plate (composition (in wt.%) of C: 0.18, P: 0.04, S: 0.05, Mn: 0.30, Si: trace, and Fe: balance) with an area of approximately 4.2 cm2 was the test specimen and functioned as the cathode during electrodeposition, while the anode was a Sn plate.

The electrochemical techniques studied were open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and potentiodynamic polarization curve. The electrochemical cells comprise a working electrode (coated mild steel plate), a platinum wire with a large area as a counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode. Electrochemical measurements were carried out using an Autolab PGSTAT 128 N potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm AUTOLAB, São Paulo, SP, Brazil). Data was processed by NOVA 2.1 and Origin 9.0 software.

The sample remained in a 3.5% NaCl solution to stabilize the OCP for 24 h. The EIS measurements were obtained as a function of the OCP from 100 KHz to 10 mHz with a sinusoidal disturbance equivalent to 10 mV. The potentiodynamic polarization curves were performed in the range of −200 to 200 mV to OCP with a scan rate of 0.166 mVs−1.

The functional groups of the CDs were characterized using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy – FTIR (Nicolete spectrometer, model 6700FT-IR) with detector TGS-KBR (UFRJ Multiuser Laboratory, RJ, Brazil). The range used was 400–4000 cm−1. The particle size distribution, electrophoretic mobility, and conductivity were evaluated, respectively, by Dynamic Light Scattering – DLS (Anton Paar Litesizer 500, Multidisciplinary Research Center – UFRJ, RJ, Brazil) and Zeta Potential – ζ (Multidisciplinary Research Center – UFRJ, RJ, Brazil). Absorption and emission in UV-VIS were characterized, respectively, by UV-VIS Spectroscopy with observation region of 200–900 nm (SHIMADZU UV2600, UFRJ Multiuser Laboratory, RJ, Brazil) and Fluorimetry (SHIMADZU RF-5301 PC, Multidisciplinary Research Center – UFRJ, RJ, Brazil).

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, a drop of the carbon quantum dot (CQD) dispersion was deposited directly onto a lacey carbon-coated copper grid (Cu grid). The sample was analyzed using a JEOL JEM-2100 Plus transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The morphology of the coatings was evaluated by Scanning Electron Microscopy – SEM with 10 kev voltage source (Magellan 450 - Materials Metrology Division - Inmetro, RJ, Brazil), Atomic Force Microscopy - AFM (Materials Metrology Division - Inmetro, RJ, Brazil) operated in tapping mode (Si Multi 75 Al tip K = 3 N/m and f = 62 KHz), the RMS (Root Mean Square) value was calculated by the JPK Image processing software and X-ray diffraction – XRD operated with Cu Kα radiation (0.1542 nm) and nickel filter with a scanning rate of 0.02° s−1 at 2θ intervals from 5° to 80°. The composition was evaluated by Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy – EDS (Magellan 450 - Division of Materials Metrology - Inmetro, RJ, Brazil) and X-Ray Excited Photoelectron Spectroscopy – XPS (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi) with 5 × 10−9 mbar pressure, AlKα monochromatic X-Ray radiation source (1486.6 eV), hemispherical analyzer and Avantage software.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analyzed the CDs to characterize functional groups. Fig. 1 A shows the spectra of citric acid and CDs. It is possible to observe that the CDs present a similar spectrum to citric acid. In the profile of CDs, the bands corresponding to groups O–H (3500 cm−1) and CO (1700 cm−1) are more intense than in the profile of citric acid. The CD spectrum presents the CC (∼1500 cm−1) band characteristic of the graphitic nucleus, which does not appear in the citric acid spectrum. The larger band of O–H seen in the CD spectrum can be related to the reduction of citric acid. Other bands stretching of groups C–H (∼2500 and ∼1400 cm−1) and C–O (1086 cm−1) can also be seen [12]. UV-VIS absorption and the fluorescence emission spectra determined the CDs' optical properties in aqueous dispersion. Fig. 1B shows the UV-VIS absorption spectrum of CD diluted in water. It can be seen three bands centered at 344, 286, and 200 nm. The bands at 200 nm correspond to the CC π - π∗ transition, which can be attributed to the presence of conjugated systems. This band corroborates the CC band seen in FTIR, indicating the presence of a graphitic core. The bands 344 and 286 nm correspond to CO n - π∗ and π - π∗ transitions, respectively, characteristic of CO bands. This confirms the presence of oxygenated groups already indicated by the FTIR profile. As seen by Aydin et al., 2022, this discrete shoulder may be related to charring time [22]. To evaluate the influence of the Sn bath on the optical properties of the CDs, the concentrations (0.03, 0.04, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 g L−1) of CDs were studied in water (Fig. 1C) and in the Sn bath (Fig. 1D). The bath components reduced the absorption of CDs, as indicated by the lower absorption intensity compared to the aqueous medium. This behavior can be determined by the interaction of the metal with the CDs. According to Tian et al., 2021, the oxygenated groups can coordinate with the empty d orbitals of the metal, forming a stable ion-CD complex, which could be related to the decreased absorption intensity [23]. Fig. 1E shows the emission spectrum of CDs when excited at 350 nm. The CDs exhibit a typical broad fluorescence emission spectrum in water. As can be seen, the emission is a single broad peak varying from 360 to 660 nm, with a maximum centered at 457 nm, indicating that the CD size dispersion is large. Further, due to the synthesis's procedure, this band can be associated with recombining excitons (electrons and holes) generated by different defects (CO groups) on the CD surface, which is corroborated by the C–O signaling in XPS analysis at 287.01 eV [1]. Fig. 1F and G shows the images of the pure Sn solution and CD solution exposed to UV light. Only the CD solution emits blue light, typical of this material class.

The size distribution and surface charge were evaluated by DLS and Zeta potential, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the composites presented varying hydrodynamic diameters, with sizes of 1 nm and dimensions of 10000 nm, corroborating the broad fluorescence emission spectrum shown in Fig. 1E. CDs are colloidal particles that tend to agglomerate. Its stability depends on the interaction between electrostatic attraction and repulsion forces. The attraction force is related to the particle's van der Waals interaction, and the repulsion depends on the electrical double layer formed between the particle and the dispersant. Thus, in a stable system, repulsion forces will predominate over those of attraction. Considering this, particle size heterogeneity is related to cluster formation due to van der Waals forces. The Zeta Potential results can be seen in Fig. 2B and Table 2 ; the average value found was −9.4 ± 3.4 mV. Furthermore, there was an average conductivity of 25.7 ± 1.2 ms cm−1 and electrophoretic mobility of −0.753 ± 0.26 μm cm V s−1. The solution composed of CDs 0.05 g L−1 stands out, which presented the highest value of Zeta Potential (−9.9 mV) and electrophoretic mobility (−0.772 μm cm V s−1). The higher electrical potential value indicates greater repulsion of the electrical double layer, which may be related to the charged surface groups, contributing to better dispersion [24].

XRD, SEM, and AFM evaluated crystalline and morphology. Fig. 3 compares the XRD profile of the Sn coating and the 0.05 g L−1 CD composite. In the Sn profile, the peaks were observed at 2θ of 30.54, 32.21, 43.71, 44.84, 55.25, 63.68, 64.51, and 80.0°, which correspond respectively to (020), (011), (220), (121), (031), (112), (231), (051) planes. In contrast, the 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite presented only the 2θ peaks 30.54, 32.21, 43.71, 44.84, 63.68, and 64.51°corresponding to the (020), (011), (220), (121), (112) e (231) planes [25]. The presence of these planes indicates the formation of the face-centered tetragonal system of Sn (β-Sn) in both cases. It is also noteworthy that in the Sn coating, the plane that occurs with greater intensity is (011), while in the composites, they were (220), (121), and (231). This result indicates a change in the growth preference of the crystallographic planes. Notably, the grains' size and contour are related to corrosion resistance. Higher energy planes have more atoms per bond that can be broken. This characteristic is responsible for accelerating the corrosion rate [13,26,27]. Even though the (121) and (231) planes appeared with high intensity in the composite material, they did not negatively influence the formation of the crystals. This result may be due to the predominance of the (220) plane, which is a low-energy plane.

The SEM of these coatings also suggests crystallographic modification by adding CDs in bath deposition, as verified by XRD results. Fig. 4 shows a uniform film with more homogeneous crystals (Fig. 4D) for the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite. On the other hand, in the Sn coating, a heterogeneous film is marked with larger crystals (Fig. 4F) and a predominance of Sn in dendritic form. These larger crystals were seen in other surface areas during the analysis. Therefore, it is believed to deal with tin crystals in tetrahedral form.

Despite having several properties, the property that makes a carbon material classified as a carbon quantum dot is its size. Thus, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is a powerful tool, not only to confirm the particle size of the material but also to give us information about the crystallinity in the material since analysis of quantum dots by XRD can be a challenge due to their size. Fig. 5 A and B presents the TEM analysis and particle size histogram, respectively. As can be seen, the material produced has an average size of 4.79 nm. Lattice fringes can be identified with a spacing of 0.197 nm (inset on Fig. 5A) corresponding to graphitic carbon (102) planes [28,29].

From this, it can be inferred that the addition of CDs changed the microstructure of the crystals. The addition of this material in optimal conditions acted as nucleation points, attracting tin particles and promoting the growth of crystals in lower energy planes, resulting in more uniform and symmetrical grains. In this way, the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite film formed a more effective “barrier” against chloride permeation, improving corrosion resistance. The topography and roughness of the Sn film and 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite were evaluated by 3D AFM images (Fig. 6 ). The Sn film presented δrms = 264 ± 60 nm, while the 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite gave δrms = 102 ± 23 nm. These results endorse the theory that the addition of CDs modified the crystallographic growth of the film, promoting a more homogeneous and uniform film, which positively impacts anti-corrosion resistance.

Table 3 shows the chemical composition obtained by EDS of Sn and Sn - 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite coatings. The results confirm the presence of Sn for both samples. It is observed that the Sn coating presents a higher percentage of oxygen (21.1%) than the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 composite (6.7%). This may indicate that the Sn coating suffered greater atmospheric oxidation than the composite, suggesting that the CDs may be protecting the Sn in the sample.

Fig. 7 A and D shows the XPS spectra for the composite Sn – CD 0,05 g L−1 and pure Sn film, respectively. In the pure Sn film, the high-resolution spectrum of Sn highlights the contribution of two different Sn 3d species with binding energies at 484.92 and 486.36 eV, corresponding to Sn0 and Sn+4, respectively (Fig. 7E). The presence of various oxidation states and the O1s peak in the sample survey indicates oxidation of the film, which was expected due to contact with the environment. In addition to O1s, the pure Sn survey also shows the presence of C1s. The high-resolution spectrum of this carbon can be observed in Fig. 7F. Four different species of C1s contribute with binding energies at 289.5, 288.5, 286.5, and 285.2 eV, corresponding to O–CO, CO, C–O, and C–C, C–H. In Fig. 7F, the peaks 289.5 and 288.5 eV overlap. These energies correspond to the Adventitious Carbon (AdC), a surface-absorbed carbon. According to Grey et al., 2024, this carbon comes from the environment and is adsorbed to the surface, predominantly aliphatic. The work studies different carbon sources and demonstrates that the more oxidized the carbon is, the more displaced it will be to the higher energy regions where the peaks will be. Therefore, the peak related to C1s may be due to the environment and the additives present in the bath [30].

The spectrum of the Sn - CD 0.05 g L−1 composite for Sn at high resolution showed a different profile from that of the pure Sn film, indicating the contribution of a single Sn 3d species with binding energy equal to 485.6 eV, which may refer to Sn–C (Fig. 7B) [31]. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows peaks with binding energies at 283.83, 285.02, and 287.01 eV, indicating the contribution of three different species (Fig. 7C). The literature reports that in cases of nanomaterials, it is common for binding energies to present values lower than the purely graphitic one (284.36 eV). However, the study conducted by Cheng et al., 2019 with Sn and graphene anodes reports this peak of 283 eV as a C–Sn bond. It is possible that both peaks overlapped. Therefore, it is suggested that the energy of 283.3 eV corresponds to C–Sn, Csp2 [31]. Lesiak et al., 2018 highlight that the separation value between the sp2-sp3 peaks can assume a difference between 0.6 and 1.35 eV. Considering the energies found (283.83 and 285.02 eV) and the difference between them (1.19 eV), it is suggested that the energy 285.02 eV belongs to sp3 hybridization. Considering the contribution of C1s with binding energy at 287.01 eV and the O1s peak (530.0 eV) in the composite survey, which presented greater intensity than in the pure film survey, it can be concluded that the peak at 287.01 eV refers to C–O bonds [32–34].

The OCP analysis of the coated mild steel in 3.5% NaCl solution for 24 h. The stable open circuit potential values can be evaluated in Table 4 . It is observed that the OCP value increases up to a concentration of 0.05 g L−1 of CDs in solution. Notably, at concentrations of 0.07 and 0.09 g L−1 , the OCP values were more negative than Sn's, becoming more cathodic. These values may be related to the formation and dissolution of tin oxides, as considered by Gupta and Srivastava, 2022. Furthermore, Fashu et al., 2015 also observed this decrease in OCP, highlighting more negative potential. It is related to the dissolution of the passive film, increasing the corrosion rate [27,35,36] (see Table 5 ).

The potentiodynamic polarization curve is shown in Fig. 8 E. For the Sn – 0.03 g L−1 CDs, Sn – 0.07 g L−1 CDs, and Sn – 0.09 g L−1 CDs composites, the Ecorr is more displaced towards the cathodic region, assuming more negative values than Sn. The Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs performed more anodic potentials than Sn. Furthermore, it is possible to suggest that the addition of CDs did not change the mechanism of cathodic reactions since the shape of the cathodic curve remains constant. As seen in Fig. 8E, region I is associated with the reduction of oxygen (reaction 1) and region II with the reduction of water (reaction 2) [37,38].
(reaction 1) O2 + 2H2O + 4e → 4 OH−
(reaction 2) 2H2O + 4e → 2 OH− + H2

About the anodic region, valleys corresponding to Sn passivation were observed for Sn – 0.03 and 0.05 g L−1 CDs composites, emphasizing the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite, which presented a more prominent valley. Evaluating the profile found in the anodic region and knowing that Sn is a metal that passivates, the passivated film is more stable for the Sn – 0.03 and 0.05 g L−1 CDs composites. Fig. 8F shows three distinct regions: III, IV, and V, which correspond to the active, passive, and transpassive regions, respectively.

The Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite presents a more extensive potential range in region III, delaying the metal's trans passivity. This result suggests excellent thermodynamic stability, compatibility, and adhesion of the film, performing a better-passivated coating among all the conditions studied. This analysis corroborates the morphological characteristics (Fig. 4). Thus, some anodic reactions are suggested to explain regions III (active region) and IV (passive region). According to the Pourbaix diagram for the Sn–H2O system, the stable compound at more anodic potentials is SnO2. Therefore, the following anodic reactions are suggested [27,38,39].
Sn + 2 OH− → Sn(OH)2 + 2e
Sn(OH)2 → SnO + H2O
SnO + H2O + 2OH− → Sn(OH)4 + 2e
Sn(OH)4 → SnO2 + 2H2O

It is essential to note in polarization curves that there are two groups of coatings, one of which decreases the cathodic current density but increases the anodic current densities (0.07 and 0.09 g L−1 CDs), dislocating the polarization curves to more negative potentials and the other which decreases the anodic current density and practically keeps the cathodic current densities (0.04 and 0.05 g L−1 CDs), dislocating the curves to more positive potentials. This different behavior could be related to different morphology and composition of the composite coatings. Both SEM and EDS analyses show differences between these two groups of coating. The SEM image and EDS analyses of the composite with 0.09 g L−1 CDs show a less homogeneous coating with a higher carbon and oxygen content (Table S1) than the composite with 0.05 g L−1 CDs (Fig. 4C and Table 3). These differences are probably responsible for the different behavior seen in polarization curves.

The EIS measurements of the coated mild steel by Sn and Sn – CDs composite films in 3.5% NaCl solution can be observed using the Nyquist and Bode diagrams in Fig. 8A–D. The electrochemical parameters were fitted using an Electrochemical Equivalent Circuit (EEC). The data were tapered using the R1(CPE1(R2(CPE2R3))) nest-type circuit (Fig. 8G). R1 refers to the solution resistance, R2 is the resistance of the passivated film, and R3 is the charge transfer resistance. Due to the inhomogeneity of the surface, the capacitance of the electric double layer (Cdl) was replaced by the constant phase element (CPE). The impedance resulting from the CPE can be expressed by the equation below:
Z
C P E
=
1
Y 0
(
j ω
)
n
Where
Z
C P E
is the impedance of constant phase element,
j =
− 1
and correspond at imaginary element,
ω = 2 π f
is angular frequency wherein
f
is frequency in Hz,
Y 0
is the magnitude of CPE and
n
represents the deviation from ideal behavior falling between −1 and 1, where the value 1 represent ideal capacitor. CPE1 refers to the capacitance of the film and CPE2 refers to the double-layer capacitance [40,41]

Adding CDs improved the coating's performance, obtaining larger capacitive loops for all concentrations of CDs studied. On the other hand, it is noteworthy that from a concentration of 0.05 g L−1, there is a decrease in the coating performance, indicating that lower concentrations provide better performance for the coating. Notably, the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite obtained the higher capacitive loop among all the conditions studied, presenting higher charge transfer resistance (Rct = 13.239 Ω cm2). The Bode diagram presents the impedance module versus log frequency (Fig. 8E), in which the highest module is observed at 0.01 Hz for all the composites studied, highlighting Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs, which presented the highest module among all the conditions analyzed (13.300 Ω cm2). The maximum phase angles are considered to evaluate capacitive behavior, with the 90° angle corresponding to an ideal capacitor [16]. Fig. 8D shows the maximum phase angles for Sn and the composites studied. In all composites, there is none with purely capacitive behavior. Despite this, an increase in the angle is observed until the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite reaches the highest maximum phase angle (-Φmax = 74.9°). These results suggest that 0.05 g L−1 CD is the best working concentration; Gupta and Srivastava, 2018 in their study of Sn and graphene oxide (GO) coatings, also observed an optimum working condition. This behavior was also observed by DAMMU et al., 2023 when studying composites of Sn and carbon nanotubes [12,13]. Fig. 8B shows the impedance diagrams of the coated mild steel by Sn and Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite films after 48 h immersion in 3.5% NaCl solution. The composite sample presented larger capacitive loops than Sn, suggesting a film more resistant to oxidation-reduction reactions.

Anodic polarization of the coated mild steel with the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite was carried out to evaluate the probable corrosion products indicated by the polarization curve (Fig. 9 C). The potential was fixed at −0.49 V/SCE for 40 min. After this process, XRD was obtained to identify the presence of possible Sn oxides. Fig. 9 shows the diffractograms of Sn and Sn – 0,05 g L−1 CDs composite coatings. The peaks referring to the Sn phase appear in both diffractograms. It is noteworthy that the composite material presented more β-Sn phases. Furthermore, phases relating to the corrosion products tin oxide II (SnO) and tin oxide IV (SnO2) can be noted in the two samples studied. The pure Sn coating presented the (110) plane of SnO, (200) and (211) referring to the SnO2 phase, while the composite coating presented the (110) plane of SnO, the (211), (310) and (112) referring to the SnO2 phase. It is believed that the more remarkable recurrence of tin oxide planes may have contributed to the emergence of the most stable passive region for the Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite [12,13].

XRD analyzed the products obtained after 24 h of mild steel coated with Sn and Sn—0.05 g L−1 CDs composite in contact with the electrolyte solution at OCP. Fig. 10 A shows the diffractogram for the composite coating. Peaks referring to the β - Sn are observed at 2θ of 32.21, 43.71, 44.84, 55.25, 63.68, 64.51, and 80° corresponding to the planes (011), (220), (121), (031), (040), (231), (051). Also, the peak 2θ at 29,74° corresponds to the plane (113) of SnO2. Compared with the profile of pure Sn (Fig. 10B), it is observed that the planes (113), (011), (040), and (231) presented peaks with different intensities, with the planes (113) and (231) more intense for the Sn - 0.05 g L−1 CDs composite. This inversion of preference in the planes may have formed a more corrosion-resistant film [22].

It is interesting to note that some crystals were observed in the SEM (Fig. 4) for the Sn and Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs samples, and that oxygen was detected by EDS and XPS, which may suggest that these crystals are oxides. However, the XRD of the samples (Fig. 3) did not show peaks related to tin oxides (SnO and SnO2), as shown in Figs. 9 and 10. At first this may seem incongruent; however, it should be remembered that XRD is a technique that identifies crystals; therefore, amorphous structures will not be detected. Considering this, it is possible to state that the crystals observed in the SEM (Fig. 4) are Sn crystals, since only β – Sn peaks were observed in the XRD (Fig. 3). The oxygen detected by EDS (Table 3) and XPS (Fig. 7) is certainly due to the non-crystalline formation of the oxides formed when exposed to air. Interestingly, when the Sn and Sn – 0.05 g L−1 CDs samples were exposed to overpotential conditions (Fig. 9) and to the corrosive medium (Fig. 10), the XRD detected tin oxide phases, suggesting that these conditions forced a reorganization of the structure, forming oxide crystals, mainly, in the composite sample as already discussed in the text.

This study proposed to investigate whether the addition of CDs positively influences the corrosion resistance of the tin coating. In this context, five concentrations of CDs (0.03, 0.04, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 g L−1) were evaluated, which were added to the tin bath. Lower concentrations of CDs (0.03, 0.04, and 0.05 g L−1) were observed to perform better anticorrosive action than high concentrations. However, it is worth noting that the concentration of 0.05 g L−1 of CDs presented the best performance among all samples studied, with more excellent charge transfer resistance (Rct = 13.2 kΩ cm2), greater impedance modules (13.3 kΩ cm2) and phase angle (-Φmax = 74.9°). This trend was already expected since the literature reports optimal working conditions for other carbon allotropes. For future work, there is a prospect of optimizing the results obtained by studying different CD precursors and new synthesis methodologies, given that these parameters can alter the size of this particle, changing its properties and possibly performing coatings different from those obtained in this study.

Taiane Fíngolo Duarte: Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, and original manuscript. Victor Magno Paiva: Methodology. Braulio Soares Archanjo: Morphological analysis of the surface (SEM). Clara Muniz da Silva de Almeida: Topographic analysis (AFM). Natasha Midori Suguihiro: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Rodrigo José Corrêa: Fluorimetry. Emerson Schwingel Ribeiro: Assistant Supervisor. Eliane D'Elia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, writing, principal supervisor, project administrator.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the collaborations with the multi-user laboratories at the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro for the availability of equipment to perform FTIR, Zeta potential, DLS, fluorimetry, XRD, and XPS analyses. Also noteworthy is the collaboration established with Inmetro, RJ - Brazil through Bráulio Soares Archanjo and Clara Muniz da Silva de Almeida, who made the morphological and topographic analyses. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of selective atomic clustering, surface texture and grain boundary constitution on the corrosion behaviour of electrodeposited Sn-xBi coatings
Corrosion Sci

Tu, “Electrodeposition and characterization of Zn-Sn alloy coatings from a deep eutectic solvent based on choline chloride for corrosion protection,” Surf. Interface Anal., vol. 47, no. 3, pp. 403-412, 2015, doi: 10.1002/sia.5728.

December 2023, p. 159845, 2024, doi: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.159845.
